backup_api_paths: GET /snapshots/ (lists snapshot object IDs; default repo path is / so the type dir sits at root)
GET /keys/ (lists key file IDs - which master-key blobs guard the repo)
GET /locks/ (lists active locks - reveals live backup activity)
GET /data/ (lists deduplicated data pack IDs; v2 returns {name,size})
HEAD /config (200 OK == a repository exists at this path; cheapest unauth existence probe)
GET /config (returns the encrypted repo config blob: version + id + chunker_polynomial after decryption, opaque on the wire)
ai_scope_discriminator: HARD GATE - restic is a fully client-side-encrypted store, so the REST server is a blind blob host: /snapshots/ returns only SHA-256 object IDs, /config is an encrypted blob, and no path/hostname/tag metadata is ever exposed server-side. You CANNOT confirm AI/ML content from the wire (that is the whole point of restic, unlike Borg/MLflow/vector DBs). In-scope confirmation is therefore strictly external and metadata-only: (1) reverse-DNS / TLS cert CN / Shodan org on the host resolves to an AI/ML operator (e.g. *.ml, training-cluster, mlflow, *-gpu, a known model lab); (2) co-located services on the same IP are AI/ML (aimap fingerprints an adjacent MLflow/Ollama/vector DB on 5000/11434/8080 etc) and restic on :8000 is the backup tier for that stack; (3) the operator's public repo/IaC or docker-compose ties the rest-server volume to a model/training/embedding directory. If none of these hold, the host is generic-substrate backup and OUT of Cat-13 AI scope - do not tag it AI/ML on the backup endpoint alone. The encrypted snapshot list is NOT evidence of AI data.
fingerprint_markers: Content-Type response header value application/vnd.x.restic.rest.v2 (or .v1) on a list/object response - restic-protocol-unique, appears on no other service
HEAD /config -> 200 with a tiny body == an initialized restic repo present at that path
GET /snapshots/ -> JSON array of 64-hex-char strings (v1) or array of {"name":"<64hex>","size":N} (v2) - all object names are exactly 32-byte SHA-256 hex
401 with WWW-Authenticate: Basic realm="restic" when .htpasswd auth is enforced (negative marker: confirms platform but gated)
Prometheus /metrics endpoint present when --prometheus set (rest_server_* metric names) corroborates rest-server identity
fp_guards: Any generic Go/nginx/Apache dev server on :8000 returns HTML or a 404 page, NOT application/vnd.x.restic.rest.* - require that exact Content-Type token, never match on http.title or bare port:8000 (8000 is a very noisy dev/admin port: Django runserver, Splunk Web, SonarQube, Portainer agent, etc.)

rclone serve restic implements the SAME REST API and emits the same vnd.x.restic.rest content type - it is a true positive for the restic backup protocol but a DIFFERENT binary; disambiguate via Server header (rclone vs rest-server / Go-http-server) and the /metrics shape

A 200 on GET /snapshots/ alone is not proof of unauth read on a real repo: an empty or non-repo path returns []; require non-empty 64-hex IDs AND a 200 on HEAD /config to call it a populated reachable repo

Catch-all/honeypot pages that echo arbitrary paths will 200 everything - guard by requiring the response body to be a strict JSON array of 64-char hex strings (or {name,size} objects), reject HTML or any non-hex token
401/403 means surface-open-access-not-exercised, NOT a finding - do not count auth-gated hosts as unauth

cves.desc: Directory traversal in rest-server < 0.11.0: a username containing a slash (e.g. foo/config) under --private-repos escapes its repo dir to read or delete another user's config/objects; CVSS 6.5, fixed 0.11.0 by restricting usernames to [unicode/0-9/-./@].
URL-encoded slash bypass of --private-repos path restriction (e.g. /foo/../bar) let one authenticated user reach another repo's objects; superseded by the 0.11.0 username-validation hardening.

pivot_paths: HEAD /config -> confirm a repo exists at the default path (200) without pulling any blob
GET /snapshots/ -> enumerate snapshot object COUNT and IDs only (names are the finding; count = how many restore points exist)
GET /index/ and GET /locks/ -> index count gauges repo size; a present lock = a backup job is live right now (operator-activity signal, read-only)
GET /keys/ -> count of key files = how many passphrases/master-key slots guard the repo (more keys = shared/multi-user repo)

If --private-repos is suspected, probe HEAD /<likely-username>/config for sibling repos by name only (enumerate existence, never traverse with a slash-username - that is the CVE write primitive, off-limits)
Correlate :8000 restic with aimap fingerprints on the same IP to attribute the backup to an AI/ML stack - all read-only, no object bytes fetched
restraint_note: Names and counts ONLY. The snapshot/index/data IDs and their count are the finding; that is the full extent of permitted enumeration. GET on any /data/<id>, /snapshots/<id>, /index/<id>, /keys/<id>, or /config BODY pulls an actual (encrypted) repo blob and is exfiltration - forbidden even though it would decrypt to nothing without the passphrase. Any POST/PUT (object upload, repo init) or DELETE is a write/destroy primitive and is forbidden. The slash-username directory-traversal CVE is a write/cross-tenant primitive - document its applicability from the version banner, never fire it. Stop at HEAD /config + the array of IDs from GET /<type>/.
